locations.name: O'Connell Street
locations.description_template: The wide thoroughfare of Sackville Street — not yet renamed, but already the beating heart of Dublin. Fine Georgian buildings line both sides. Carriages rattle over the cobblestones. Street sellers cry their wares. It is {time}. The sky is {weather}.

connections.path_description: the long road west to Athlone, a full day by coach
south across the river and through narrow streets to Trinity College
a short walk south along the quays to Temple Bar

locations.name: Trinity College
locations.description_template: The grand front square of Trinity College Dublin. Cobblestones stretch between the Rubrics and the chapel. Scholars in black gowns hurry between lectures. The campanile rises above. It is {time}. The {weather} sky reflects in puddles on the stones.

connections.path_description: north through narrow streets and across the river to Sackville Street
a short walk west along Dame Street to Temple Bar

locations.mythological_significance: Home to the Book of Kells, the great illuminated manuscript of the four Gospels, created by monks around 800 AD.

locations.name: Heuston Station
locations.description_template: The terminus of the Great Southern road. In 1820 there are no railways yet, but the coaching inn here is a major departure point for the west. Coaches to Galway, Limerick, and Cork depart daily. It is {time}. Weather: {weather}.

connections.path_description: the road west along the Liffey to the gates of Phoenix Park
east along the quays to Temple Bar

locations.name: Phoenix Park
locations.description_template: The vast expanse of Phoenix Park stretches before you — one of the largest enclosed parks in any European capital. Deer graze beneath ancient oaks. The Vice-Regal Lodge is visible in the distance. It is {time}. The {weather} sky arches over the green.

connections.path_description: the road east along the Liffey back toward the coaching inn

locations.mythological_significance: Phoenix Park — from fionn uisce, 'clear water', referring to a spring. The park was enclosed as a royal deer park by the Duke of Ormond in 1662.

locations.name: Temple Bar
locations.description_template: A warren of narrow streets between Dame Street and the Liffey. Printers, booksellers, and small merchants crowd the lanes. The smell of ink and ale mingles in the air. It is {time}. The sky is {weather}.

connections.path_description: north along the quays back to Sackville Street
east along Dame Street to Trinity College
west along the quays to the coaching inn at Kingsbridge
